course_title: Self-Compressing Neural Networks: Learning to Quantize from Scratch
course_description: Reproduce the Self-Compressing Neural Networks paper (arXiv 2301.13142) from scratch using PyTorch. Build a neural network that learns its own compression during training by making quantization bit-widths differentiable per-channel parameters. Starting from quantization fundamentals, progress through the straight-through estimator, build a quantization-aware convolutional layer (QConv2d), wire up the compression penalty loss, train on MNIST, and reproduce the reference result: ~98% accuracy at ~18KB model size from 87,860 original parameters — a ~20x compression from 32-bit floats. The final module explores the Pareto frontier of the compression-accuracy tradeoff and reveals emergent channel pruning.

modules.title: Quantization Fundamentals — From Floats to Fixed-Point
modules.description: Before making quantization learnable, you must understand what quantization IS. This module builds intuition for how floating-point weights are mapped to fixed-point integers with a given bit-width and scale factor (exponent). You will implement quantize/dequantize functions, explore how bit-width controls precision, and see firsthand why naive rounding without proper scaling destroys information — motivating the per-channel exponent parameter e used in the paper.
learning_objectives: Implement quantization as scaling by 2^(-e), clamping to [-2^(b-1), 2^(b-1)-1], and rounding
Implement dequantization as rescaling by 2^e to restore original dynamic range
Explain how the number of bits b controls precision: b bits gives 2^b distinct representable values
Explain why a per-channel exponent (scale factor) is necessary for useful quantization
Measure quantization error (MSE, max absolute error, SNR) for different bit-widths and exponents

exercises.title: Quantize and Dequantize: The Building Blocks
exercises.description: Implement the two fundamental operations of quantization: converting a floating-point tensor to a fixed-point integer representation with a given bit-width b and exponent e, then converting it back. The quantization formula from the paper is: q(x, b, e) = 2^e * round(clamp(2^(-e) * x, -2^(b-1), 2^(b-1)-1)). You implement the quantize step (scale, clamp, round) and the dequantize step (rescale by 2^e) as separate functions.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block (torch, numpy, math), helper function print_quantization_table() that formats a table of reconstruction errors, realistic weight generation using Kaiming uniform initialization, and a complete __main__ test harness that calls quantize/dequantize for bit-widths [1, 2, 3, 4, 8] with multiple exponents, prints formatted reconstruction error tables, and asserts that errors decrease with more bits.
exercises.what_student_writes: quantize(x, num_bits, exponent) — scale by 2^(-exponent), clamp to signed b-bit integer range [-2^(b-1), 2^(b-1)-1], round to nearest integer (~8-12 lines); dequantize(qx, exponent) — multiply by 2^exponent to restore original scale (~3-5 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints a table showing bit-width vs reconstruction MSE for Kaiming-initialized weights. MSE decreases as bits increase: 1-bit MSE is large, 8-bit MSE is near zero. Asserts that 8-bit reconstruction error < 1e-4.
exercises.key_insight: Clamping to [-2^(b-1), 2^(b-1)-1] defines the signed integer range for b bits. The exponent e controls which portion of the real number line maps to this discrete integer grid — choosing e poorly wastes most of the quantization range.
exercises.common_mistakes: Off-by-one in upper clamping bound (should be 2^(b-1)-1, not 2^(b-1)); using unsigned range [0, 2^b-1] instead of signed; forgetting to round after clamping; applying exponent with wrong sign (2^e instead of 2^(-e) for quantization)

exercises.title: Bit-Width Explorer: Precision vs. Compression
exercises.description: With working quantize/dequantize functions, explore how bit-width and exponent interact. For each (bit-width, exponent) pair, compute reconstruction MSE, max absolute error, number of unique quantized values, and signal-to-noise ratio. Complete code for quantization is provided — you implement the statistics computation and observe the patterns.
exercises.what_is_provided: Complete quantize/dequantize from Exercise 1, matplotlib visualization code (heatmap of MSE vs bit-width and exponent, histogram of quantized value distributions), realistic Conv2d weight generation, complete __main__ block that runs the full analysis and saves figures.
exercises.what_student_writes: compute_quantization_stats(weights, exponents, bit_widths) — for each (exponent, bit_width) pair: quantize then dequantize, compute MSE, max_abs_error, num_unique_values, and SNR_dB, return nested dict (~15-20 lines); find_optimal_exponent(weights, num_bits) — sweep exponents to find the one minimizing MSE for given bit-width (~8-10 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints a 2D table of MSE indexed by (exponent, bit-width). Student observes: (1) each additional bit roughly halves MSE, (2) there is an optimal exponent for each bit-width, (3) 2 bits is extremely aggressive compression. Saves a heatmap figure.
exercises.key_insight: For each bit-width there exists an optimal exponent that minimizes quantization error. The paper initializes e = -8.0 because typical neural network weights have magnitudes around 2^(-8) to 2^(-3) after Kaiming initialization.
exercises.common_mistakes: Computing SNR without handling the zero-error case (division by zero); using linear rather than log scale for exponent sweep; forgetting that SNR is 10*log10(signal_power/noise_power)

exercises.title: Naive Rounding vs. Proper Quantization
exercises.description: See why naive rounding (just round weights to nearest 0.25, 0.5, etc.) fails spectacularly compared to properly scaled quantization. First implement the naive approach, observe its poor performance, then use the proper quantize/dequantize with optimal exponent and compare. This motivates why the exponent parameter e is critical and must be learned.
exercises.what_is_provided: Complete proper quantize/dequantize, a pretrained simple 2-layer MLP on MNIST (weights loaded from a small checkpoint), MNIST test data loading, accuracy evaluation function, and __main__ block that runs both approaches and prints side-by-side comparison.
exercises.what_student_writes: naive_quantize(weights, num_bits) — round weights to nearest value on a uniform grid spanning [min(weights), max(weights)] with 2^num_bits steps (~8-12 lines); evaluate_both(model, test_data, bit_widths) — for each bit-width, apply naive and proper quantization to all model weights, evaluate accuracy, print comparison table (~15-20 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints a comparison table: at 2 bits, naive quantization drops accuracy to ~40-60% while proper quantization with optimal exponent maintains ~85-90%. At 4 bits, proper quantization is near-lossless while naive still shows significant degradation.
exercises.key_insight: Naive rounding fails because it ignores the actual weight distribution — most weights cluster near zero but the grid spans the full [min, max] range, wasting precision on the tails. The exponent parameter e adapts the quantization grid to match the weight distribution.
exercises.common_mistakes: Using the same grid for all layers (different layers have different weight distributions); forgetting to handle the case where min == max; not computing the grid step size correctly for 2^num_bits levels

inline_questions.question: If a weight has value 0.037 and the quantization grid has step size 0.03125 (2^(-5)), what are the two nearest grid points? How much error is introduced by rounding to each?
inline_questions.context: After implementing quantize(), test your understanding of how the grid spacing relates to the exponent.

inline_questions.question: With 2 bits you can represent 4 distinct signed integer values: {-2, -1, 0, 1}. If the exponent e = -8, what floating-point values do these map to after dequantization?
inline_questions.context: After implementing dequantize(), understand the full quantize-dequantize pipeline.

inline_questions.question: The paper initializes b = 2.0 (2 bits per weight). For a network with 87,860 weights, what is the model size in bytes at 2 bits/weight? Compare to 32-bit floats.
inline_questions.context: Understanding the compression ratio that motivates the paper.

modules.visible_outcome: Student can quantize and dequantize weight tensors, measure reconstruction error, and has seen firsthand that proper scaling (the exponent parameter) is critical for useful quantization. The comparison table showing naive vs. proper quantization makes the case for learnable exponents.
key_excerpts:
self.e = Tensor.full((out_channels, 1, 1, 1), -8.)
self.b = Tensor.full((out_channels, 1, 1, 1), 2.)  # start with 2 bits per weight
This work focuses on reducing neural network size, which is a major driver of neural network execution time, power consumption, bandwidth, and memory footprint.

modules.title: The Straight-Through Estimator — Gradients Through Rounding
modules.description: The core mathematical trick that makes self-compression possible. Rounding is a piecewise-constant function with zero gradient almost everywhere, which blocks backpropagation. The Straight-Through Estimator (STE) provides a proxy gradient: in the forward pass, we round normally; in the backward pass, we pretend rounding never happened and pass the gradient straight through. This module implements the STE from scratch in PyTorch and verifies that gradients flow correctly through the quantization operation.
learning_objectives: Explain why round() has zero gradient almost everywhere and why this blocks learning
Implement the STE pattern: (qw.round() - qw).detach() + qw
Verify that the forward pass produces rounded values while the backward pass passes gradients through
Demonstrate that parameters before the STE receive nonzero gradients despite the rounding operation
Apply the STE to optimize a learnable parameter through a quantization bottleneck

exercises.title: Why Rounding Kills Gradients
exercises.description: First, try to optimize a simple learnable scale parameter through naive rounding — watch the gradients vanish and optimization fail completely. Then apply the STE pattern and observe optimization succeed. This exercise builds visceral understanding of WHY the STE is needed before asking you to implement it.
exercises.what_is_provided: Complete setup: a learnable scale parameter (nn.Parameter), a target tensor, loss computation (MSE between rounded(scale * input) and target), optimizer setup, training loop structure, gradient logging, and __main__ block that runs both the naive and STE approaches for 200 steps each, printing loss curves and gradient magnitudes.
exercises.what_student_writes: naive_round_forward(x, scale) — multiply by scale, apply torch.round(), return result (~3-5 lines); ste_round_forward(x, scale) — multiply by scale, apply STE pattern (qw.round() - qw).detach() + qw, return result (~4-6 lines); train_and_log(forward_fn, steps) — training loop that logs loss and gradient magnitude at each step (~12-15 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints two columns: naive (loss stuck, gradients zero after step 1) vs STE (loss decreasing, gradients nonzero throughout). The naive approach fails to reduce loss below ~0.3 while STE converges loss below 0.01.
exercises.key_insight: torch.round() has zero gradient because it is piecewise constant — the derivative of a staircase function is zero between steps and undefined at steps. The STE bypasses this by pretending rounding did not happen during the backward pass.
exercises.common_mistakes: Forgetting .detach() in the STE pattern (this makes the rounding differentiable but with WRONG gradients, not zero gradients); confusing the STE output value (which IS rounded) with the gradient (which ignores rounding)

exercises.title: Implement and Verify the STE
exercises.description: Implement the STE as a clean, reusable PyTorch function and verify it behaves correctly: forward pass equals round(), backward pass passes gradients through as identity. Use torch.autograd.gradcheck-style verification to confirm correctness numerically.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, a StraightThroughRound class skeleton inheriting from torch.autograd.Function with @staticmethod forward/backward signatures, a functional wrapper ste_round(), test vectors with known expected forward outputs and gradient values, and __main__ block that runs forward correctness tests, gradient flow tests, and a numerical gradient verification.
exercises.what_student_writes: StraightThroughRound.forward(ctx, x) — save x for backward, return x.round() (~3-4 lines); StraightThroughRound.backward(ctx, grad_output) — return grad_output unchanged (~2-3 lines); ALSO implement the equivalent one-liner: ste_round_inline(x) = (x.round() - x).detach() + x (~1-2 lines); verify_gradient_flow(x) — create a computation graph through ste_round, compute loss, backprop, check that x.grad is nonzero and matches expected values (~8-12 lines)
exercises.milestone: All tests pass: (1) forward output matches torch.round() exactly, (2) backward gradient matches identity (grad_output passed through unchanged), (3) both implementations (autograd.Function and inline) produce identical results. Prints 'ALL STE TESTS PASSED'.
exercises.key_insight: The expression (x.round() - x).detach() + x has value x.round() in the forward pass (because detach doesn't affect values) but gradient equal to the gradient of x in the backward pass (because .detach() stops gradient flow through the (x.round() - x) term, leaving only the gradient through the final + x).
exercises.common_mistakes: In the autograd.Function version, forgetting to save tensors for backward (ctx.save_for_backward); returning the wrong number of gradients from backward; not understanding that .detach() only affects the computation graph, not the tensor values

exercises.title: STE with Learnable Quantization Parameters
exercises.description: Apply the STE to a realistic scenario: a learnable exponent parameter e that controls quantization scale. Given a fixed weight tensor and a target (the same weights quantized at an optimal exponent), optimize e via gradient descent through the STE. This previews how e and b will be learned in the full QConv2d layer.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, fixed weight tensor (realistic Conv2d weights), target tensor (weights quantized at known optimal exponent), ste_round() function from Exercise 2, optimizer setup (Adam, lr=0.1), and __main__ block that trains for 500 steps, prints e convergence trajectory, and verifies e converges near the known optimal value.
exercises.what_student_writes: quantize_with_learnable_e(weights, e, num_bits) — compute 2^(-e) * weights, clamp to b-bit range, apply ste_round(), multiply by 2^e (~6-8 lines); training_loop(weights, target, num_bits, steps) — initialize e as nn.Parameter, optimize with Adam, at each step: quantize, compute MSE loss vs target, backprop, step, log e value (~15-20 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints e value at each step — starts at initial guess (e.g., 0.0), converges to near the optimal exponent (e.g., -8.0 for Kaiming-initialized weights). Final MSE < 1e-6. Prints 'Learned e = {value:.4f}, target e = {target:.4f}, converged: True'.
exercises.key_insight: The STE enables gradient-based optimization of parameters that affect the quantization grid. Even though the quantized weights are discrete, the gradient with respect to e flows through the STE, allowing e to be learned. This same mechanism will enable learning b (bit-width) in the full self-compression setup.
exercises.common_mistakes: Initializing e too far from the optimal value (gradient descent may get stuck in a bad local minimum); using too high a learning rate (e oscillates); forgetting to apply relu to b in the clamping range (not needed here since b is fixed, but easy to confuse)

inline_questions.question: What is the derivative of round(x) at x = 2.3? At x = 2.5? At x = 3.0? Why does this make gradient-based optimization of parameters before rounding impossible?
inline_questions.context: Before implementing the STE, reason about why the naive approach fails mathematically.

inline_questions.question: In the expression (qw.round() - qw).detach() + qw, trace through both the forward and backward pass for qw = 2.7. What value is computed forward? What is d(output)/d(qw)?
inline_questions.context: After implementing the STE, verify your understanding of the detach trick.

inline_questions.question: The STE introduces a biased gradient estimate — the true gradient of round() is 0 almost everywhere, but we pretend it is 1. Why does this work in practice despite being mathematically incorrect?
inline_questions.context: After completing all three exercises, reflect on the theoretical justification.

modules.visible_outcome: Student has implemented the STE in two forms (autograd.Function and inline one-liner), verified gradient flow numerically, and used the STE to optimize a learnable exponent parameter through quantization. The contrastive exercise showing naive rounding failure vs STE success provides visceral understanding of why this trick is essential.
key_excerpts: The Straight-Through Estimator redefines the rounding function derivative, enabling optimization with respect to quantization parameters b (bit depth) and e (exponent).
Basic STE used throughout instead of pseudo-quantization noise

modules.title: Building the QConv2d Layer — Learnable Quantization
modules.description: The heart of self-compression: a custom convolutional layer where each output channel has its own learnable exponent (e) and bit-width (b) parameters alongside standard weights. The forward pass quantizes weights using the STE, and qbits() reports the effective compression. This module builds QConv2d from scratch, piece by piece: first the quantized weight computation (qweight), then the full forward pass with STE, then the bits-counting method, and finally gradient verification.
learning_objectives: Implement qweight(): per-channel quantization with learnable e and b using broadcasting
Implement the QConv2d forward pass: quantize via qweight(), apply STE, convolve with dequantized weights
Implement qbits(): count the effective number of bits contributed by each channel
Verify that gradients flow to all three parameter types: weight, e, and b
Understand why relu(b) ensures non-negative bit-widths and how b <= 0 produces zero-bit channels (pruning)

exercises.title: Per-Channel Quantized Weights (qweight)
exercises.description: Implement the qweight() method that computes quantized weights using per-channel e and b parameters. The key formula is: qw = clamp(2^(-e) * weight, -2^(relu(b)-1), 2^(relu(b)-1)-1). The e and b tensors have shape (out_channels, 1, 1, 1) to broadcast across the (in_channels, kH, kW) dimensions of each output channel's weights.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block (torch, math), QConv2d class skeleton with __init__ that creates weight, e, b parameters with correct shapes and initialization (e=-8.0, b=2.0), Kaiming uniform weight initialization, and __main__ block that creates a QConv2d(3, 16, 3), calls qweight(), verifies output shape matches weight shape, checks that output values are integers (within tolerance), and prints min/max/mean statistics.
exercises.what_student_writes: qweight(self) — compute quantized weights: (1) apply relu to self.b to get effective bits, (2) compute lower bound -2^(eff_b - 1), (3) compute upper bound 2^(eff_b - 1) - 1, (4) scale weights by 2^(-self.e), (5) clamp to [lower, upper] bounds, return result (~8-12 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints qweight statistics: shape matches (16, 3, 3, 3), all values are within the expected integer range for 2-bit quantization [-2, 1], and the quantized values cluster around a small number of distinct integers. Prints 'qweight shape: correct, range: correct, unique values per channel: ~4'.
exercises.key_insight: relu(b) ensures bit-widths are non-negative. When b goes below zero during training, relu(b) = 0, making both bounds equal to 0 (since -2^(-1) = -0.5 rounds to 0 and 2^(-1) - 1 = -0.5), which forces all quantized weights in that channel to zero — this is emergent pruning.
exercises.common_mistakes: Forgetting to apply relu to b before computing bounds (negative b produces imaginary numbers); broadcasting error — e and b must be (out_channels, 1, 1, 1) to broadcast over (out_channels, in_channels, kH, kW); computing upper bound as 2^(b-1) instead of 2^(b-1)-1 (off by one)

exercises.title: QConv2d Forward Pass with STE
exercises.description: Implement the complete QConv2d forward pass: (1) compute quantized weights via qweight(), (2) apply STE to round the quantized weights while preserving gradient flow, (3) dequantize by multiplying by 2^e, (4) convolve the input with the dequantized weights using F.conv2d. This is the key code path from the reference implementation.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, QConv2d class with __init__ and qweight() from Exercise 1, ste_round() function from Module 1, torch.nn.functional for conv2d, and __main__ block that creates a QConv2d(1, 32, 5), feeds a batch of random 28x28 images through it, verifies output shape is correct, checks that output requires_grad is True, and prints output statistics.
exercises.what_student_writes: forward(self, x) — (1) get quantized weights via self.qweight(), (2) apply STE: w = ste_round(qw), note this is (qw.round() - qw).detach() + qw, (3) dequantize: dw = (2 ** self.e) * w, (4) return F.conv2d(x, dw) (~6-10 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints: output shape (batch, 32, 24, 24) is correct, output requires_grad=True, output statistics (mean, std) are in reasonable range. Asserts that the forward pass produces finite, non-zero outputs. Prints 'QConv2d forward pass: PASSED'.
exercises.key_insight: The dequantized weight is 2^e * ste_round(qw). In the forward pass, this produces 2^e * round(qw) — a discretized weight. In the backward pass, STE makes the gradient flow as if the weight were 2^e * qw (continuous), enabling gradient descent on weight, e, and b simultaneously.
exercises.common_mistakes: Applying 2^e BEFORE rounding instead of after (this changes the quantization grid); forgetting stride/padding parameters in F.conv2d (not needed for basic version but easy to trip on); confusing the quantized integer qw with the dequantized float 2^e * round(qw)

exercises.title: Counting Bits: The qbits() Method
exercises.description: Implement qbits() which computes the total number of bits used by a QConv2d layer. Each output channel contributes relu(b_i) * fan_in bits, where fan_in = in_channels * kernel_h * kernel_w. This is the metric that the compression loss will minimize. Also implement a model-level function that computes average bits per weight Q across all QConv2d layers.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, QConv2d class with __init__, qweight(), and forward() from previous exercises, a simple model class with 3 QConv2d layers, and __main__ block that creates the model, computes per-layer qbits and model-level Q, converts to model size in bytes, and prints a summary table.
exercises.what_student_writes: qbits(self) — compute total bits: relu(self.b).sum() * fan_in, where fan_in = product of self.weight.shape[1:] (~4-6 lines); compute_avg_bits_per_weight(model) — sum qbits() across all QConv2d layers, divide by total weight count, return Q (~8-12 lines); compute_model_bytes(Q, weight_count) — convert average bits per weight to total bytes: Q * weight_count / 8 (~2-3 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints per-layer table: 'Layer | out_ch | fan_in | qbits | bits/weight'. With b=2.0 initialization, each channel contributes 2*fan_in bits. Total model Q should be ~2.0 bits/weight. Model size at Q=2.0 = 87860 * 2 / 8 ≈ 21,965 bytes. Prints 'Model size: ~21.5 KB at 2.0 bits/weight'.
exercises.key_insight: qbits() must use relu(b), not raw b, because negative bit-widths are meaningless — relu ensures channels with b <= 0 contribute zero bits. The compression loss will push b toward zero for unimportant channels, and relu(b) ensures a smooth transition from 'active' to 'pruned'.
exercises.common_mistakes: Using raw b instead of relu(b) in the sum (negative bits make no physical sense); computing fan_in incorrectly (it's the product of all weight dimensions EXCEPT the output channel dimension); forgetting that model_bytes = Q/8 * weight_count, not Q * weight_count

exercises.title: Gradient Flow Verification
exercises.description: Verify that gradients flow correctly to all three parameter types (weight, e, b) in the QConv2d layer. Create a simple test: forward pass through QConv2d, compute a scalar loss, backpropagate, and check that weight.grad, e.grad, and b.grad are all non-zero. This is a critical sanity check before building the full training loop — if any gradient is zero, self-compression cannot work.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, complete QConv2d class from previous exercises, a simple loss function (MSE between output and random target), and __main__ block structure that creates a layer, runs forward, computes loss, and has assertion scaffolding.
exercises.what_student_writes: verify_gradient_flow(layer, input_tensor) — (1) forward pass, (2) compute scalar loss, (3) loss.backward(), (4) check layer.weight.grad is not None and has nonzero entries, (5) check layer.e.grad is not None and has nonzero entries, (6) check layer.b.grad is not None and has nonzero entries, (7) print gradient statistics (mean, std, fraction nonzero) for each parameter, return True if all pass (~18-25 lines); analyze_gradient_magnitudes(layer) — compare relative magnitudes of weight, e, and b gradients, discuss implications for learning rate choices (~8-12 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints a gradient report for each parameter: 'weight.grad — mean: X, std: Y, frac_nonzero: 1.0; e.grad — mean: X, std: Y, frac_nonzero: 1.0; b.grad — mean: X, std: Y, frac_nonzero: 1.0'. All three parameters have nonzero gradients. Prints 'GRADIENT FLOW VERIFIED: all parameters receive gradients'.
exercises.key_insight: All three parameters (weight, e, b) must receive gradients for self-compression to work. Weight gradients drive task performance, e gradients adapt the quantization scale, and b gradients drive compression by reducing bit-widths. If b.grad were zero, the network could never learn to compress itself.
exercises.common_mistakes: Forgetting to zero gradients before backward pass (stale gradients from previous tests); not checking for None vs zero gradients (None means the parameter was not part of the computation graph at all, which is a bug); running in torch.no_grad() context accidentally

inline_questions.question: The e and b parameters have shape (out_channels, 1, 1, 1) while weights have shape (out_channels, in_channels, kH, kW). How does PyTorch broadcasting handle the operation 2^(-e) * weight? Which dimension is shared, and which are broadcast?
inline_questions.context: Understanding broadcasting is essential for implementing qweight() correctly.

inline_questions.question: When relu(b) = 0, what are the clamping bounds? Trace through: lower = -2^(0-1) = -2^(-1) = -0.5, upper = 2^(0-1) - 1 = -0.5. What does clamping to [-0.5, -0.5] and then rounding produce?
inline_questions.context: Understanding why b <= 0 effectively prunes a channel.

inline_questions.question: The reference implementation uses different learning rates for quantization parameters (0.5) vs weights (10^-3). Given the gradient magnitudes you observed in Exercise 4, why might this be necessary?
inline_questions.context: After analyzing gradient magnitudes, think about optimization dynamics.

modules.visible_outcome: Student has a complete, tested QConv2d layer with qweight(), forward(), and qbits() methods. Gradient flow is verified for all three parameter types. The layer is ready to be used in a full self-compressing CNN.
key_excerpts:
self.weight = Tensor.uniform(out_channels, in_channels, *self.kernel_size, low=-scale, high=scale)
self.e = Tensor.full((out_channels, 1, 1, 1), -8.)
self.b = Tensor.full((out_channels, 1, 1, 1), 2.)  # start with 2 bits per weight
z_l = I_l * H_l * W_l * sum(i=1 to O_l) b_{l,i}

modules.title: Self-Compression Training on MNIST
modules.description: Wire everything together: build a 5-layer self-compressing CNN, implement the compression regularization loss L = L_task + lambda * Q, and train on MNIST for 20,000 steps. The key insight is that adding lambda * Q to the cross-entropy loss creates a smooth pressure toward smaller bit-widths. During training, you will watch some channels have their bit-widths driven to near zero while the network maintains high accuracy. This module reproduces the reference result: ~98.2% accuracy at ~18KB model size.
learning_objectives: Implement the compression regularization loss: L = L_task + lambda * Q where Q = avg bits per weight
Build a 5-layer self-compressing CNN architecture matching the reference implementation
Train the model for 20,000 steps and track both accuracy and model size over time
Reproduce the reference result: ~98% accuracy at ~18KB from 87,860 parameters
Understand how lambda controls the compression-accuracy tradeoff

exercises.title: The Compression Loss
exercises.description: Implement the self-compression loss function that combines task loss with a compression penalty. The combined loss is L = L_task + lambda * Q, where L_task is cross-entropy and Q is the average bits per weight across all QConv2d layers. Verify that the compression term creates gradient pressure to reduce bit-widths by checking that b.grad is negative when lambda > 0.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, complete QConv2d class from Module 2, a small test model with 2 QConv2d layers, random input/target data, cross-entropy loss computation, and __main__ block that computes loss with different lambda values and prints how b gradients change direction as lambda increases.
exercises.what_student_writes: compute_compression_term(model) — iterate over model's QConv2d layers, sum qbits(), divide by total weight count to get Q (~8-12 lines); self_compression_loss(model, logits, targets, lam) — compute cross-entropy task loss, compute Q via compute_compression_term, return (task_loss + lam * Q, task_loss_value, Q_value) as a tuple (~6-8 lines); verify_compression_gradient(model, lam_values) — for each lambda, compute loss, backprop, check sign and magnitude of b.grad (~10-15 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints table: 'lambda | Q | task_loss | total_loss | mean(b.grad)'. At lambda=0, b.grad direction is determined only by task loss. At lambda=0.05, b.grad is negative (compression pressure). At lambda=1.0, b.grad is strongly negative. Prints 'Compression gradient verified: higher lambda -> stronger b reduction pressure'.
exercises.key_insight: The compression term Q = avg bits/weight is differentiable with respect to b (through relu). The gradient d(Q)/d(b) is positive (more bits = higher Q), so adding lambda * Q to the loss creates NEGATIVE gradient on b — pushing bit-widths toward zero. Lambda controls the strength of this compression pressure.
exercises.common_mistakes: Computing Q without dividing by total weight count (makes lambda scale-dependent on model size); forgetting that the compression term must be differentiable (using .item() too early breaks the graph); using sum instead of mean for Q computation

exercises.title: Train the Self-Compressing CNN
exercises.description: Build the full self-compressing CNN architecture (5 QConv2d layers: 1->32->32->64->64->10, with BatchNorm and max pooling) and train it on MNIST for 20,000 steps with the self-compression loss (lambda=0.05). Track test accuracy and model size at every evaluation step. This is the capstone implementation that reproduces the paper's result.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block (torch, torchvision, tqdm), complete QConv2d class, MNIST dataset loading with appropriate transforms, a get_test_accuracy() evaluation function, a dual-axis plotting function (accuracy + model size over steps), and __main__ block that trains the model and saves the final plot.
exercises.what_student_writes: SelfCompressingCNN class — __init__ defining 5 QConv2d layers (1->32 k5, 32->32 k5, 32->64 k3, 64->64 k3, 576->10 k1), BatchNorm layers, forward() with relu, max_pool2d, and flatten (~25-35 lines); train_step(model, optimizer, images, labels, lam) — forward pass, compute self-compression loss, backward, optimizer step, return (loss, Q) (~10-15 lines); training_loop(model, train_loader, test_loader, steps, lam) — full training loop with periodic evaluation and logging of accuracy + model bytes (~20-30 lines)
exercises.milestone: Training progress bar shows loss decreasing, model bytes decreasing, accuracy increasing. After 20,000 steps: test accuracy >= 97.5%, model size <= 25,000 bytes. Prints final line: 'Final — accuracy: {acc:.2f}%, model size: {bytes:.1f} bytes, compression: {ratio:.1f}x'. Saves training dynamics plot.
exercises.key_insight: During training, the network jointly optimizes accuracy and compression. Some channels have their bit-widths driven to near zero (effectively pruned) while others stabilize at 2-4 bits. This is emergent self-compression — the network decides which channels to keep and at what precision.
exercises.common_mistakes: Forgetting to use model.train() and model.eval() modes for BatchNorm; not zeroing gradients between steps; using too large a batch size (512 in reference); incorrect reshape before final QConv2d layer (must flatten spatial dims then reshape to (B, 576, 1, 1))

exercises.title: Analyze Learned Bit-Width Distributions
exercises.description: After training, extract the learned b values from each QConv2d layer and analyze the distribution. Identify which channels were pruned (b <= 0), which retained high precision (b > 3), and which settled at moderate precision (1 < b < 3). Visualize the bit-width distribution per layer and compute layer-level compression statistics.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, complete QConv2d and model classes, a function to load trained model weights (or the training code from Exercise 2 if running end-to-end), matplotlib visualization utilities, and __main__ structure.
exercises.what_student_writes: extract_bit_widths(model) — for each QConv2d layer, extract relu(b) values as a flat numpy array, return dict of {layer_name: bit_widths} (~8-12 lines); compute_layer_stats(bit_widths_dict) — for each layer, compute: num_pruned (b <= 0.01), num_active, mean_bits, max_bits, layer_compression_ratio (~10-15 lines); visualize_bit_distributions(bit_widths_dict) — create a multi-panel figure with one histogram per layer showing the distribution of effective bit-widths (~15-20 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints per-layer analysis: 'Layer 0: 32 channels, 5 pruned, 27 active, mean_bits=2.31, max_bits=3.52'. Histogram shows bimodal distribution: cluster near 0 (pruned) and cluster around 2-3 (active). Total print: 'Network learned to prune X of Y channels, averaging Z bits/weight for remaining channels'. Saves distribution figure.
exercises.key_insight: The learned bit-width distribution is NON-UNIFORM — the network assigns different precision to different channels based on their importance for the task. Channels detecting simple features (e.g., constant backgrounds) get pruned, while channels detecting complex features (e.g., digit strokes) retain 2-4 bits.
exercises.common_mistakes: Using raw b values instead of relu(b) for analysis (negative b should be treated as 0 bits); not accounting for the difference between b as a parameter and effective bits after relu; plotting raw b instead of relu(b) in histograms

inline_questions.question: If lambda is set to 0 (no compression penalty), what happens to the bit-widths during training? Do they increase, decrease, or stay the same?
inline_questions.context: Understanding the role of the compression regularization term.

inline_questions.question: The reference implementation uses lambda=0.05. If you increase lambda to 0.5, would you expect the final model to be smaller or larger? More or less accurate? Why?
inline_questions.context: Predicting the effect of the compression coefficient before experimenting.

inline_questions.question: The paper mentions 'irreversible forgetting' — if a channel's bit-width drops to 0 and is pruned, can it be reactivated later in training? What does relu(b) imply about this?
inline_questions.context: After analyzing the bit-width distributions, consider the training dynamics.

modules.visible_outcome: Student has a fully trained self-compressing CNN that achieves ~98% accuracy on MNIST at ~18-25KB model size (~15-20x compression). The bit-width distribution visualization shows emergent non-uniform quantization with some channels pruned to zero bits. The training dynamics plot shows accuracy rising while model size drops.
key_excerpts:
Q = functools.reduce(lambda x,y: x+y, [l.qbits() for l in model.layers if isinstance(l, QConv2d)]) / weight_count
loss = loss + 0.05*Q   # hyperparameter determines compression vs accuracy

modules.title: The Pareto Frontier & Emergent Pruning
modules.description: With the full self-compression pipeline working, this module explores the compression-accuracy tradeoff systematically. By varying lambda across a range, you map out the Pareto frontier — the optimal tradeoff curve between model size and accuracy. You also compare self-compression (quantization-aware training) against naive uniform post-training quantization to see why joint optimization during training outperforms post-hoc compression. Finally, you perform a deep analysis of emergent channel pruning to understand what the network learns to discard.
learning_objectives: Map the Pareto frontier by training with different lambda values and plotting size vs. accuracy
Compare self-compression against uniform post-training quantization at the same compression level
Analyze which channels are pruned in each layer and hypothesize about their function
Understand the tradeoff between compression coefficient, convergence speed, and final accuracy
Explain why quantization-aware training outperforms post-training quantization

exercises.title: Map the Size-Accuracy Pareto Frontier
exercises.description: Train the self-compressing CNN with multiple lambda values: [0.001, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5] and collect the final (model_size_bytes, test_accuracy) pair for each. Plot the Pareto frontier. Observe how the curve is smooth and how lambda=0.05 sits at a sweet spot for MNIST.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, complete SelfCompressingCNN class and training loop from Module 3 (encapsulated as train_self_compressing(lam, steps) returning (accuracy, model_bytes)), matplotlib plotting, and __main__ structure.
exercises.what_student_writes: sweep_lambda(lam_values, steps_per_run) — for each lambda value, train a fresh model, record final (accuracy, model_bytes), return list of results (~12-18 lines); plot_pareto_frontier(results) — scatter plot of model_bytes (x) vs accuracy (y), annotate each point with its lambda value, connect with a line, label axes (~12-15 lines); find_pareto_optimal(results) — identify which (lambda, accuracy, size) points are Pareto-optimal (no other point has both better accuracy AND smaller size) (~8-12 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints a table: 'lambda | accuracy | model_bytes | pareto_optimal?'. Plot shows a smooth curve: low lambda -> high accuracy, large model; high lambda -> lower accuracy, tiny model. Lambda=0.05 achieves >98% accuracy at ~18KB. Saves Pareto frontier figure. Prints 'Pareto-optimal configurations: [...]'.
exercises.key_insight: The Pareto frontier is smooth and concave — there is no free lunch, but the tradeoff curve is favorable. The paper achieves floating-point accuracy with as few as 3% of the bits. Small changes in lambda near the 'knee' of the curve produce large changes in model size with minimal accuracy impact.
exercises.common_mistakes: Not training each lambda run from a fresh random initialization (reusing weights biases the comparison); using too few training steps for high lambda values (they converge slower because compression fights accuracy); plotting accuracy on x-axis instead of model size

exercises.title: Self-Compression vs. Uniform Post-Training Quantization
exercises.description: Train a standard (non-quantized) CNN to ~99% accuracy on MNIST, then apply uniform post-training quantization at various bit-widths (1, 2, 3, 4, 8). Compare the resulting (size, accuracy) points against the self-compression Pareto frontier. Self-compression should dominate: better accuracy at every model size.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, standard CNN class (same architecture but with regular Conv2d), MNIST data loading, training function for the standard CNN, the self-compression Pareto results from Exercise 1 (or code to regenerate), and __main__ structure with side-by-side plotting.
exercises.what_student_writes: uniform_quantize_model(model, num_bits) — for each weight tensor in the model, find optimal per-layer exponent (minimize MSE), quantize all weights at that bit-width, replace in-place (~12-18 lines); evaluate_uniform_quantization(model, test_loader, bit_widths) — for each bit-width, clone model, apply uniform quantization, evaluate accuracy, compute model size, return results list (~10-15 lines); plot_comparison(self_compress_results, uniform_results) — overlay both sets of (size, accuracy) points on one plot, different colors/markers, legend (~12-15 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints comparison table: 'Method | bits | accuracy | model_bytes'. At 2 bits uniform: accuracy ~85-92%. Self-compression at ~2 bits avg: accuracy ~98%. The gap is dramatic at low bit-widths. Plot shows self-compression curve strictly above uniform quantization curve. Prints 'Self-compression outperforms uniform PTQ by {X:.1f}% accuracy at comparable model size'.
exercises.key_insight: Self-compression (quantization-aware training) outperforms post-training quantization because the model ADAPTS to quantization errors during training. The network learns which channels can tolerate low precision and which need more bits — this non-uniform allocation is impossible with uniform PTQ.
exercises.common_mistakes: Using per-channel optimal exponents for PTQ (makes PTQ look better than it is in practice — use per-layer for fair comparison); not training the baseline model to full convergence (makes PTQ look worse than it should); forgetting to clone the model before quantizing (modifying in-place corrupts the baseline)

exercises.title: Emergent Channel Pruning Deep Dive
exercises.description: Perform a detailed analysis of which channels are pruned (b <= 0) in the trained self-compressing model. For each layer, identify pruned vs. active channels, compute the weight magnitude statistics for each, and visualize the pruned channels' original weight distributions. Investigate whether the network preferentially prunes channels with small weight magnitudes or channels that are redundant with other channels.
exercises.what_is_provided: Import block, trained self-compressing model (loaded from checkpoint or trained in-script), visualization utilities for per-channel weight histograms, and __main__ structure.
exercises.what_student_writes: identify_pruned_channels(model) — for each QConv2d layer, return dict with 'pruned_indices', 'active_indices', 'b_values' (~8-12 lines); analyze_pruning_pattern(model, pruning_info) — for each layer: compute mean weight magnitude for pruned vs active channels, compute pairwise cosine similarity between pruned channel weights and remaining active channel weights, check if pruned channels are 'redundant' (high similarity to an active channel) (~20-30 lines); visualize_pruning(model, pruning_info) — multi-panel figure: (1) bar chart of b values per layer color-coded by pruned/active, (2) histogram of weight magnitudes for pruned vs active channels, (3) similarity matrix for channels in the most-pruned layer (~15-20 lines)
exercises.milestone: Prints per-layer pruning report: 'Layer 0: 5/32 pruned. Pruned channels avg weight mag: 0.023, Active avg: 0.058. Redundancy: 3/5 pruned channels have >0.8 cosine similarity to an active channel.' Final summary: 'The network prunes channels that are either low-magnitude or redundant with other channels, achieving structured sparsity as an emergent property of bit-width optimization.'
exercises.key_insight: Channels with b driven to zero are effectively pruned — this is STRUCTURED pruning as an emergent property of self-compression, not a separate pruning algorithm. The network learns which channels are dispensable: typically those with small weight magnitudes or those that are redundant (highly correlated) with other channels.
exercises.common_mistakes: Comparing pruned channel weights AFTER quantization (they're all zero by construction — must compare original float weights before compression drove b to zero); using Euclidean distance instead of cosine similarity for redundancy (magnitude differences confound the comparison); not distinguishing between 'small but useful' and 'redundant' channels

inline_questions.question: The Pareto frontier has a 'knee' — a region where small accuracy sacrifices yield large model size reductions. Where is this knee for MNIST? Would you expect it to be at the same lambda for CIFAR-10?
inline_questions.context: After plotting the Pareto frontier, think about what determines the knee location.

inline_questions.question: Why does uniform post-training quantization fail at 2 bits while self-compression succeeds? What specific adaptation does self-compression make that is impossible in the PTQ setting?
inline_questions.context: After the comparative experiment, articulate the fundamental advantage.

inline_questions.question: The paper mentions 'irreversible forgetting' as a risk of self-compression. If a channel is pruned (b -> 0) during early training and later becomes needed, can the network recover it? What does this imply about the training dynamics?
inline_questions.context: After the pruning analysis, consider the limitations of the approach.

modules.visible_outcome: Student has mapped the complete Pareto frontier for self-compression on MNIST, demonstrated its superiority over uniform PTQ with a concrete comparison plot, and performed deep analysis of emergent pruning patterns. The student can explain WHY self-compression works, not just HOW.
key_excerpts: floating point accuracy with as few as 3% of the bits and 18% of the weights remaining in the network
Compression factor gamma log-uniformly sampled from [10^-3, 10^-0.5] produces a parameterized size-accuracy tradeoff locus

When b = 0, the quantization output always equals zero, meaning weights become zero-bit representations and can be removed without changing network output. By sharing quantization parameters across entire channels, zero-bit channels can be pruned.
